result:
# The Cartography of Forgetting

I want to write about maps.

Not the functional kind—not the GPS voice telling you to turn left in three hundred feet, not the subway diagrams with their color-coded arteries, not the satellite images that have flattened every mystery out of the planet's surface. I want to write about old maps. The ones made before the world was fully known. The ones with monsters swimming in the margins.

There's a famous phrase that cartographers supposedly wrote to indicate unexplored territory: *hic sunt dracones*. Here be dragons. It appeared on the Lenox Globe, created around 1510, marking the waters off eastern Asia. What strikes me about this phrase isn't the dragons—it's the honesty of it. The cartographer looked at the edge of what he knew and rather than inventing false detail, rather than pretending confidence he didn't have, he wrote: here there is something, and I cannot tell you what it is, except that it is large and probably dangerous.

We've lost that honesty, I think. We've traded the admission of ignorance for the performance of total knowledge. Google Maps will show you satellite imagery of nearly every road on earth. You can look at the street where your grandmother grew up in a town you've never visited. You can examine the parking lot of a mall in Kuala Lumpur. The dragons are gone—not because we've slain them, but because we've replaced them with the illusion that the map and the territory are the same thing.

They are not the same thing. They have never been the same thing.

---

The philosopher Alfred Korzybski wrote "the map is not the territory" in 1931, and we've been nodding at this idea ever since without really absorbing it. We quote it the way we quote "everything happens for a reason"—as a kind of verbal tic that signals sophistication without requiring us to change how we actually move through the world.

But try this: think about the mental map you carry of your own personality. The story you tell yourself about who you are. Maybe you believe you're a patient person. Maybe you believe you're bad at mathematics. Maybe you believe you're someone who doesn't cry at movies, or someone who always finishes what they start, or someone who is fundamentally unlucky in love.

These are maps. They are not the territory.

The territory is the actual unfolding of your behavior across ten thousand moments, messy and contradictory and context-dependent. The territory is the fact that you were patient yesterday and monstrous this morning and somewhere in between all afternoon. The territory is that you failed at algebra and understood calculus intuitively. The territory resists summary. The map exists because the territory is too much—too much information, too much flux, too much to hold in conscious attention while also buying groceries and answering emails and remembering to call your mother.

Maps are a technology of reduction. They are useful precisely because they throw things away. A map that contained all information about a territory would simply be the territory. Jorge Luis Borges imagined exactly this—an empire whose cartographers made a map so detailed it was the same size as the empire itself. It was useless, of course. It rotted in the desert. The animals made their nests in it.

What I find beautiful about this image is its melancholy. The perfect map, the complete map, is indistinguishable from the thing it represents and therefore worthless as a map. To be useful, a map must lie. It must simplify, omit, flatten. The roads must be wider than they are. The mountains must be represented by small triangles. The distance between two points must be approximate.

Every map is an argument about what matters.

---

I grew up in a house that was full of atlases. My father collected them—not as investments or curiosities, but because he genuinely loved them. He would sit at the kitchen table on Sunday mornings with his coffee and turn pages through the population density of Sub-Saharan Africa or the topography of the Pacific Rim the way other people read novels. I thought this was strange when I was young. Now I think it was one of the more admirable things a person can do with a Sunday morning.

What I understand now, looking back, is that he was reading arguments. Every atlas is a vision of the world as some particular group of people wanted it to be understood. A Soviet atlas from the 1970s and an American atlas from the same decade will show you the same planet and will somehow show you completely different planets. The projections will differ. The colors will differ. The borders will differ—especially the borders will differ. The names of places will differ. The Falkland Islands or Las Malvinas, depending on who made your map. The Persian Gulf or the Arabian Gulf. The Sea of Japan or the East Sea.

Places have the names powerful people gave them. Maps are drawn in the shapes that suit the nations that commission them. The famous Mercator projection, which most of us grew up with, dramatically enlarges the landmasses of the northern hemisphere relative to the southern. Greenland appears comparable in size to Africa. In reality, you could fit roughly fourteen Greenlands inside Africa. The Mercator projection was designed for navigation—it preserves angles, which is useful for sailing—but it has the secondary effect of making Europe and North America look more significant, more central, more *there* than the equatorial regions.

We learned to see the world as the projection told us to see it. And then we made political and economic and moral decisions that reflected that learned seeing.

The map tells you what matters. You internalize the map. You act from the map. The territory suffers the consequences.

---

Here is what I think about most often when I think about maps: the coastlines of the past.

Go back far enough and the earth looks almost unrecognizable. During the Last Glacial Maximum, about twenty thousand years ago, sea levels were roughly four hundred feet lower than they are today. The coastlines of the world were substantially different. Britain was connected to Europe by a landmass geologists call Doggerland—a broad plain of forest and wetland and river valley that is now the floor of the North Sea. Indonesia was a peninsula rather than an archipelago. Australia, Tasmania, and New Guinea formed a single continent.

People lived in these places. They built fires, they raised children, they developed languages, they developed relationships with the specific landscape that surrounded them—a landscape that is now underwater. All of that is gone. Whatever they understood about the shape of their world, whatever maps they held in their minds, whatever landmarks meant home to them—all of it drowned as the glaciers melted and the waters rose, slowly but inexorably, over thousands of years.

I find this almost unbearable to think about. Not the loss of the people—they survived, mostly, retreating to higher ground over generations, adapting—but the loss of the knowledge. The intimate geographical knowledge that takes generations to accumulate. Every fisher who knew which underwater formation would hold fish in which season. Every herbalist who knew which hillside held which plant at which time of year. Every elder who could close their eyes and walk from the river to the village to the burial ground in complete darkness because the map in their mind had been inscribed by a lifetime of walking.

That knowledge is simply gone. The landscape it described is under water. The people who held it are long dead. We have no record of any of it. It's not lost the way a library burns—with a specific moment of destruction, a clear before and after. It dissolved. It attenuated across generations, each one knowing slightly less than the last, until there was no one left who remembered.

This has happened many times. This will happen again.

---

I want to talk about a different kind of map now.

Neuroscientists who study memory sometimes use the word "engram" to describe the physical trace that a memory leaves in the brain—the specific pattern of neural connections that, when activated, produces the experience of remembering. For most of the twentieth century, engrams were theoretical. We knew memory had to be stored somehow, somewhere, in some physical form, but we couldn't see it. Karl Lashley spent decades trying to find the location of memory in rat brains and concluded, somewhat desperately, that memories appeared to be stored everywhere and nowhere at once.

Now we have better tools. We can watch specific neurons light up. We can identify the cells that respond to specific experiences. We can, in a limited way, see the map.

What strikes me about the engram is this: it is not a recording. It is a reconstruction. When you remember something, your brain doesn't play back a stored file. It reassembles the experience from its component traces—and in reassembling it, it subtly changes it. The act of remembering is also the act of rewriting. Every time you recall a memory, you slightly alter it, leaving a new trace that is a combination of the original experience and the context of this particular remembering. The memory you have of your tenth birthday has been shaped by every time you've thought about your tenth birthday since.

You are not the keeper of your past. You are its ongoing editor.

This means the map of your personal history is not a stable document. It is a palimpsest—a surface written over again and again, each new layer partially obscuring the last, until what you have is a complex stratigraphy of traces that bears only a complicated relationship to whatever actually happened. The woman you remember your mother being is a character you've been writing for decades, not a person you've been accurately recording.

And yet this is not a flaw, exactly. The brain consolidates memory during sleep, pruning and organizing, connecting new experiences to old frameworks, building the kind of structured understanding that allows for generalization. You couldn't function if you remembered everything with perfect fidelity. You'd be overwhelmed. You'd be a Borges character—his Funes el memorioso, who after an accident gains perfect memory and is completely incapacitated by it, unable to think because unable to forget, unable to generalize because unable to discard.

The forgetting is a feature. The distortion is a feature. The map is useful because it lies in the right ways.

---

What I keep circling back to is the question of what we lose when we make maps and what we lose when we lose the maps.

There are navigator cultures in the Pacific—the Polynesian and Micronesian peoples who crossed open ocean in outrigger canoes centuries before European sailors had developed the technology to do what those sailors considered impossible. They navigated by stars, yes, but also by wave patterns. They could feel, with their bodies, the way swells rebounded off invisible islands below the horizon. They could read the color of water and the behavior of birds and the configuration of clouds. Some navigators would lie on the floor of the canoe to feel the swells more directly through their spine.

They made maps, too—but some of those maps were not meant to be consulted on the water. They were teaching tools. The stick charts of the Marshall Islands, made from coconut fronds and shells, represented ocean swells and island positions in a form that could be studied on land, memorized, and then left behind. The map was a scaffold for building the internal knowledge. Once you knew, you didn't need the map. The map was a stage in learning, not a permanent crutch.

I think about this every time I notice I can't remember phone numbers anymore. I've outsourced that knowledge to my phone, and the phone has a map of who people are by their numbers that I no longer hold. If the phone dies, I can't call anyone. I have no scaffold. I have only dependency.

This is the Socratic worry about writing—that it would produce people who appeared to know things but did not know them, who had access to information without the formation that comes from having actually internalized it. Plato recorded this argument in the *Phaedrus*, which is its own delightful paradox—recording in writing an argument against the reliability of written records.

---

I don't want to end on a note of pure lamentation. The nostalgia for lost knowledge, for embodied skills, for ways of knowing that have been eroded by technology—it's a real feeling, but it can curdle into something unproductive. The stick chart navigators weren't navigating in paradise. They were navigating in an extremely dangerous ocean in small boats with no rescue services and significant mortality. The people of Doggerland were not living in prelapsarian bliss. They were doing hard, cold, uncertain work to stay alive.

What I want to preserve is not the hardship. It's the honesty.

The Lenox Globe cartographer who wrote *hic sunt dracones* was being honest about the limits of knowledge. The Polynesian navigator who memorized wave patterns with their body was acknowledging that some knowledge resists external storage. Karl Lashley, stumbling toward the conclusion that memory was somehow everywhere and nowhere, was doing science the way science is supposed to be done—following the evidence into deep uncertainty and reporting from there.

We live in a time that is embarrassed by uncertainty. The algorithm gives you recommendations rather than asking you what you want. The GPS tells you where to turn rather than asking where you're trying to go. The map is always complete; the territory is never more than the map represents.

Here be dragons. We've forgotten that this was an honest statement, not a failure. The space between what we know and what exists—that gap, that honest blank space—is not a problem to be eliminated. It is the condition of inquiry. It is the thing that makes the next discovery possible. It is, if you're willing to see it that way, the most interesting part of the map.

The monsters were never the danger. The danger was always in believing the map was finished.

---

My father still sits with atlases sometimes, though he's older now and the Sunday mornings have a different quality to them. I asked him once what he was actually looking for, all those years of turning pages. He thought about it for a long time—genuinely thought about it, which was characteristic of him.

"I'm not looking for anything," he said. "I'm looking at how people decided what to show."

That's it. That's the whole thing. The map is never the territory. The map is always the mapmaker.

And the mapmaker was always a person, working at the edge of the known, deciding what mattered, drawing the boundary where the knowledge ended, and then—honestly, if they were doing their best work—writing something true in the margin.

Something large. Something unmeasured. Something worth the crossing.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
